title.en: The Data Model: Dunder Methods That Make Objects Pythonic

description.en: Python is protocol-oriented: the interpreter calls 'dunder' (double-underscore) methods on your objects to make them behave like built-ins. Learn the data model — construction and display, equality and hashing, containers and iteration, operator overloading, context managers, and callables — and why duck typing via dunders, not inheritance, is the heart of Pythonic design.

story_variants.en:
## The Interpreter Is Calling Your Methods

When you write `len(x)`, `x + y`, `x == z`, `for item in x`, or `with x:`, you are not using fixed, built-in machinery that only works on built-in types. You are triggering a **protocol** — and Python responds by calling a specially named method *on your object*. `len(x)` calls `x.__len__()`. `x + y` calls `x.__add__(y)`. `for` calls `x.__iter__()`. These specially named methods — wrapped in double underscores — are called **dunder** methods, and together they form Python's **data model**.

This is the single most important idea in the language's design: Python is **protocol-oriented**, not inheritance-oriented. To make your object iterable you do not inherit from some `Iterable` base class — you implement `__iter__`. To make it addable, you implement `__add__`. The interpreter never asks "what type is this?" It asks "does this object support the protocol I need?" That is **duck typing**: if it implements the right dunders, it walks and quacks like the built-in.

> **Key idea:** A dunder method is a hook. You don't call it directly — you implement it, and the interpreter calls it for you when the matching syntax or built-in is used. Your objects become Pythonic by *speaking the protocols*, not by inheriting them.

## Construction and Display

Three dunders cover an object's birth and how it shows itself:

- `__init__(self, ...)` runs *after* the object is created, to initialize its attributes. (The object is actually allocated by `__new__`, which you rarely touch.)
- `__repr__(self)` returns the **unambiguous, developer-facing** string — ideally something you could paste back into Python. This is what the REPL and `repr()` show, and the fallback for everything.
- `__str__(self)` returns the **readable, user-facing** string for `print()` and `str()`. If you omit it, Python falls back to `__repr__`.

A good rule: always write `__repr__`; add `__str__` only when end-users need something prettier.

## Equality and Hashing — The Linked Pair

`__eq__(self, other)` defines what `==` means for your object. But equality has a partner you must not forget: `__hash__`. An object's hash decides which bucket it lands in inside a `set` or as a `dict` key. The contract is strict: **equal objects must have equal hashes.**

Here is the trap that surprises everyone. The moment you define `__eq__`, Python **sets `__hash__` to `None`**, making your object *unhashable* — you can no longer put it in a set or use it as a dict key. This is a safety feature: Python refuses to guess a hash that stays consistent with your new equality. If your object is immutable and you want it hashable, you must define `__hash__` yourself, typically `return hash((self.x, self.y))` over the same fields you compare.

## Containers and Iteration

| Protocol | Dunder(s) | Triggered by |
|----------|-----------|--------------|
| Length | `__len__` | `len(x)` |
| Indexing / slicing | `__getitem__` | `x[i]`, `x[a:b]` |
| Membership | `__contains__` | `item in x` |
| Iteration | `__iter__` (+ `__next__`) | `for v in x` |
| Equality | `__eq__` | `x == y` |
| Hashing | `__hash__` | `set`, `dict` keys |
| Addition | `__add__` | `x + y` |
| Context manager | `__enter__`, `__exit__` | `with x:` |
| Callable | `__call__` | `x(...)` |

Implement `__len__` and `__getitem__` and your object already behaves like a sequence — `for` will even iterate it (by calling `__getitem__` with `0, 1, 2, ...`) if there is no `__iter__`. Add `__iter__` for proper, explicit iteration.

## Operator Overloading

`__add__`, `__sub__`, `__mul__`, `__lt__`, `__gt__`, and friends let your type respond to `+`, `-`, `*`, `<`, `>`. The convention that keeps you sane: arithmetic dunders should **return a new object**, never mutate `self`. `Vector(1, 2) + Vector(3, 4)` should hand back a fresh `Vector(4, 6)`, leaving both operands untouched — exactly how `int` and `str` behave.

## Context Managers and Callables

`__enter__` and `__exit__` power the `with` statement: `__enter__` runs on entry (its return value is bound by `as`), and `__exit__` runs on the way out — *guaranteed*, even if an exception is raised — which is why `with open(...)` always closes the file.

`__call__` makes an *instance* callable like a function. After you define it, `obj()` runs `obj.__call__()`. This is how objects can carry state yet be invoked like plain functions — the backbone of decorators, stateful callbacks, and partial-application objects.

Learn the data model and a quiet truth emerges: there is very little "magic" in Python. `len`, `+`, `in`, `for`, `with`, `()` — all of it is just the interpreter politely calling dunder methods it hopes your object implements. Implement them, and your class stops being a second-class citizen and becomes indistinguishable from a built-in.

left.label: Define __eq__ only -> __hash__ becomes None -> object UNHASHABLE

right.label: Define __eq__ AND __hash__ -> usable in sets & dict keys

description.en: Defining __eq__ silently sets __hash__ to None as a safety feature. To use the object in a set or as a dict key, define __hash__ over the same fields you compare. Contract: equal objects MUST hash equal.

steps.description: __add__ is the hook for '+'. It builds and returns a NEW Vector — it never mutates self.
__eq__ defines '==' by comparing the (x, y) tuples field-by-field.
__repr__ returns 'Vector(x, y)' — the unambiguous, paste-back-able form.

output: len(x), x+y, x==y, for v in x, with x:, x() -> all call DUNDERS.
Python is protocol-oriented: duck typing via dunders, not inheritance.
Defining __eq__ kills __hash__ -> add __hash__ to stay set/dict-usable.

instruction.en:
Make YOUR class behave like a built-in by implementing the data-model protocols. Complete the Vector class so it speaks Python's dunders:

- __init__(self, x, y): store x and y as attributes.
- __add__(self, other): return a NEW Vector whose components are the sums — never mutate self.
- __eq__(self, other): return True when both x and y match (compare the (x, y) tuples).
- __repr__(self): return the string 'Vector(x, y)', e.g. 'Vector(4, 6)'.

The demo adds two vectors and prints the result. Because __add__ returns a new Vector and print() uses __repr__, the expected output is exactly:
Vector(4, 6)

en: In __init__, bind the arguments to the object: self.x = x and self.y = y.
In __add__, build and hand back a fresh object: return Vector(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y). Returning a new object (not mutating self) is how '+' behaves for int and str.
In __eq__, compare the whole pair at once: return (self.x, self.y) == (other.x, other.y).
In __repr__, use an f-string: return f"Vector({self.x}, {self.y})". print() falls back to __repr__ when there is no __str__, so this is what gets shown.

practice_challenges.test_code:
v = Vector(1, 2) + Vector(3, 4)
assert isinstance(v, Vector), "__add__ must return a Vector"
assert (v.x, v.y) == (4, 6), f"addition wrong, got ({v.x}, {v.y})"
assert repr(Vector(4, 6)) == "Vector(4, 6)", f"__repr__ wrong, got {repr(Vector(4, 6))}"
assert Vector(1, 2) + Vector(3, 4) == Vector(4, 6), "__eq__ should make summed vector equal Vector(4, 6)"
assert Vector(1, 2) == Vector(1, 2), "equal components must compare equal"
assert not (Vector(1, 2) == Vector(2, 1)), "different components must not be equal"
a = Vector(1, 2)
b = a + Vector(0, 0)
assert a is not b, "__add__ must return a NEW object, not mutate/return self"

q.en: What happens when you write `for item in x`, where `x` is your own class instance?

en: Python checks that x inherits from a special Iterable base class
The interpreter calls x.__iter__() (or falls back to indexing via x.__getitem__) — a protocol, not inheritance
Nothing — only built-in types like list can be iterated
Python copies x into a list first

q.en: You define `__eq__` on a class but not `__hash__`, then try to put an instance in a set. What happens?

en: It works fine — Python keeps the default __hash__
Defining __eq__ sets __hash__ to None, so the object is unhashable and raises TypeError in a set
The set silently stores it but comparisons break
Python auto-generates a correct __hash__ from __eq__

q.en: What is the recommended behavior of an arithmetic dunder like `__add__`?

en: Mutate self in place and return None
Return a NEW object holding the result, leaving both operands unchanged
Modify the other operand and return it
Raise an error unless the operands are identical objects

q.en: What does implementing `__call__` on a class let you do?

en: Call the class without parentheses
Make an instance invocable like a function, so obj(...) runs obj.__call__(...)
Automatically print the object
Use the object as a dictionary key
